title: Calculus
blurb: Unit-by-unit practice for the Calculus course — limits, derivatives, and integrals, exam-style.

units.title: Limits & Continuity
units.blurb: What a function is heading toward: limits from graphs and algebra, limits at infinity, and the definition of an unbroken graph.

units.title: The Derivative
units.blurb: Secants collapse onto tangents: the difference quotient, the derivative function, the power rule, and the four library derivatives.

units.title: Differentiation Techniques
units.blurb: Product, quotient, and chain rules; combining them, and second derivatives — the whole differentiable world unlocked.

units.title: Applications of Derivatives
units.blurb: Tangent and normal lines, rise and fall, peaks and valleys certified two ways, concavity — and optimization, the art of the best.

units.title: Integrals
units.blurb: Differentiation reversed, areas by slicing, and the Fundamental Theorem that collapses infinite sums into one subtraction.

units.title: Applications of Integrals
units.blurb: Areas between curves, motion recovered from velocity, average values — and a capstone running the whole course in one arc.

forms.title: Limits by direct substitution
forms.skill: Polynomials are continuous — the limit is just the value at the point.

variants.statement: Evaluate $\lim_{x \to 2}(2x^2 + 3x - 5)$.
variants.explanation: A polynomial is continuous, so the limit is just the value at $x = 2$: substituting gives $9$. Plugging in $x = -2$ instead — a sign slip on the limit point — gives $-3$.

variants.statement: Evaluate $\lim_{x \to 1}(3x^2 - 2x + 4)$.
variants.explanation: A polynomial is continuous, so the limit is just the value at $x = 1$: substituting gives $5$. Plugging in $x = -1$ instead — a sign slip on the limit point — gives $9$.

variants.statement: Evaluate $\lim_{x \to 3}(x^2 + 4x - 3)$.
variants.explanation: A polynomial is continuous, so the limit is just the value at $x = 3$: substituting gives $18$. Plugging in $x = -3$ instead — a sign slip on the limit point — gives $-6$.

variants.statement: Evaluate $\lim_{x \to 2}(2x^2 - 5x + 3)$.
variants.explanation: A polynomial is continuous, so the limit is just the value at $x = 2$: substituting gives $1$. Plugging in $x = -2$ instead — a sign slip on the limit point — gives $21$.

variants.statement: Evaluate $\lim_{x \to -1}(3x^2 + 2x - 7)$.
variants.explanation: A polynomial is continuous, so the limit is just the value at $x = -1$: substituting gives $-6$. Plugging in $x = 1$ instead — a sign slip on the limit point — gives $-2$.

variants.statement: Evaluate $\lim_{x \to 4}(x^2 - 3x + 5)$.
variants.explanation: A polynomial is continuous, so the limit is just the value at $x = 4$: substituting gives $9$. Plugging in $x = -4$ instead — a sign slip on the limit point — gives $33$.

variants.statement: Evaluate $\lim_{x \to 1}(2x^2 + 7x - 1)$.
variants.explanation: A polynomial is continuous, so the limit is just the value at $x = 1$: substituting gives $8$. Plugging in $x = -1$ instead — a sign slip on the limit point — gives $-6$.

variants.statement: Evaluate $\lim_{x \to 2}(4x^2 - x + 6)$.
variants.explanation: A polynomial is continuous, so the limit is just the value at $x = 2$: substituting gives $20$. Plugging in $x = -2$ instead — a sign slip on the limit point — gives $24$.

variants.statement: Evaluate $\lim_{x \to -2}(x^2 + 5x - 2)$.
variants.explanation: A polynomial is continuous, so the limit is just the value at $x = -2$: substituting gives $-8$. Plugging in $x = 2$ instead — a sign slip on the limit point — gives $12$.

variants.statement: Evaluate $\lim_{x \to 3}(3x^2 - 4x + 2)$.
variants.explanation: A polynomial is continuous, so the limit is just the value at $x = 3$: substituting gives $17$. Plugging in $x = -3$ instead — a sign slip on the limit point — gives $41$.

variants.statement: Evaluate $\lim_{x \to 3}(2x^2 + x - 9)$.
variants.explanation: A polynomial is continuous, so the limit is just the value at $x = 3$: substituting gives $12$. Plugging in $x = -3$ instead — a sign slip on the limit point — gives $6$.

variants.statement: Evaluate $\lim_{x \to -3}(x^2 + 6x + 4)$.

forms.title: 0/0 limits: factor and cancel
forms.skill: Factor the numerator, cancel the vanishing factor, then substitute.

variants.statement: Evaluate $\lim_{x \to 3}\frac{x^2 - 9}{x - 3}$.
variants.explanation: Factor $x^2 - 9 = (x - 3)(x + 3)$, cancel the common factor, and substitute: $x + 3 \to 6$. Reading the $\frac{0}{0}$ form as an answer of $0$ is the classic slip.

variants.statement: Evaluate $\lim_{x \to 4}\frac{x^2 - 16}{x - 4}$.
variants.explanation: Factor $x^2 - 16 = (x - 4)(x + 4)$, cancel the common factor, and substitute: $x + 4 \to 8$. Reading the $\frac{0}{0}$ form as an answer of $0$ is the classic slip.

variants.statement: Evaluate $\lim_{x \to 5}\frac{x^2 - 25}{x - 5}$.
variants.explanation: Factor $x^2 - 25 = (x - 5)(x + 5)$, cancel the common factor, and substitute: $x + 5 \to 10$. Reading the $\frac{0}{0}$ form as an answer of $0$ is the classic slip.

variants.statement: Evaluate $\lim_{x \to 6}\frac{x^2 - 36}{x - 6}$.
variants.explanation: Factor $x^2 - 36 = (x - 6)(x + 6)$, cancel the common factor, and substitute: $x + 6 \to 12$. Reading the $\frac{0}{0}$ form as an answer of $0$ is the classic slip.

variants.statement: Evaluate $\lim_{x \to 7}\frac{x^2 - 49}{x - 7}$.
variants.explanation: Factor $x^2 - 49 = (x - 7)(x + 7)$, cancel the common factor, and substitute: $x + 7 \to 14$. Reading the $\frac{0}{0}$ form as an answer of $0$ is the classic slip.

variants.statement: Evaluate $\lim_{x \to -2}\frac{x^2 - 4}{x + 2}$.
variants.explanation: Factor $x^2 - 4 = (x + 2)(x - 2)$, cancel the common factor, and substitute to get $-4$. Reading the $\frac{0}{0}$ form as an answer of $0$ is the classic slip.

variants.statement: Evaluate $\lim_{x \to -3}\frac{x^2 - 9}{x + 3}$.
variants.explanation: Factor $x^2 - 9 = (x + 3)(x - 3)$, cancel the common factor, and substitute to get $-6$. Reading the $\frac{0}{0}$ form as an answer of $0$ is the classic slip.

variants.statement: Evaluate $\lim_{x \to -4}\frac{x^2 - 16}{x + 4}$.
variants.explanation: Factor $x^2 - 16 = (x + 4)(x - 4)$, cancel the common factor, and substitute to get $-8$. Reading the $\frac{0}{0}$ form as an answer of $0$ is the classic slip.

variants.statement: Evaluate $\lim_{x \to 5}\frac{x^2 - 7x + 10}{x - 5}$.
variants.explanation: The numerator factors as $(x - 5)(x - 2)$; cancel $x - 5$ and substitute $x = 5$ into the other factor to get $3$. Answering $0$ from the $\frac{0}{0}$ form is the classic slip.

variants.statement: Evaluate $\lim_{x \to 3}\frac{x^2 - 2x - 3}{x - 3}$.
variants.explanation: The numerator factors as $(x - 3)(x + 1)$; cancel $x - 3$ and substitute $x = 3$ into the other factor to get $4$. Answering $0$ from the $\frac{0}{0}$ form is the classic slip.

variants.statement: Evaluate $\lim_{x \to 2}\frac{x^2 + 2x - 8}{x - 2}$.
variants.explanation: The numerator factors as $(x - 2)(x + 4)$; cancel $x - 2$ and substitute $x = 2$ into the other factor to get $6$. Answering $0$ from the $\frac{0}{0}$ form is the classic slip.

variants.statement: Evaluate $\lim_{x \to -2}\frac{x^2 - x - 6}{x + 2}$.
variants.explanation: The numerator factors as $(x + 2)(x - 3)$; cancel $x + 2$ and substitute $x = -2$ into the other factor to get $-5$. Answering $0$ from the $\frac{0}{0}$ form is the classic slip.

forms.title: Limits at infinity
forms.skill: Equal degrees: ratio of leading coefficients. Bigger denominator degree: 0.

variants.statement: Evaluate $\lim_{x \to \infty}\frac{6x^2 - x + 2}{3x^2 + 4x + 5}$.
variants.explanation: The degrees match, so only the leading terms survive as $x \to \infty$: the limit is $\frac{6}{3} = 2$. Taking the ratio of the constant terms, $\frac{2}{5}$, is the classic slip.

variants.statement: Evaluate $\lim_{x \to \infty}\frac{2x^2 + 3x + 1}{4x^2 - x + 3}$.
variants.explanation: The degrees match, so only the leading terms survive as $x \to \infty$: the limit is $\frac{2}{4} = \frac{1}{2}$. Taking the ratio of the constant terms, $\frac{1}{3}$, is the classic slip.

variants.statement: Evaluate $\lim_{x \to \infty}\frac{5x^2 + 2x - 3}{2x^2 + x + 4}$.
variants.explanation: The degrees match, so only the leading terms survive as $x \to \infty$: the limit is $\frac{5}{2} = \frac{5}{2}$. Taking the ratio of the constant terms, $-\frac{3}{4}$, is the classic slip.

variants.statement: Evaluate $\lim_{x \to \infty}\frac{3x^2 - 4x + 2}{6x^2 + x + 5}$.
variants.explanation: The degrees match, so only the leading terms survive as $x \to \infty$: the limit is $\frac{3}{6} = \frac{1}{2}$. Taking the ratio of the constant terms, $\frac{2}{5}$, is the classic slip.

variants.statement: Evaluate $\lim_{x \to \infty}\frac{8x^2 + x + 3}{2x^2 - 5x + 6}$.
variants.explanation: The degrees match, so only the leading terms survive as $x \to \infty$: the limit is $\frac{8}{2} = 4$. Taking the ratio of the constant terms, $\frac{1}{2}$, is the classic slip.

variants.statement: Evaluate $\lim_{x \to \infty}\frac{4x^2 + 5x - 2}{3x^2 + 2x + 7}$.
variants.explanation: The degrees match, so only the leading terms survive as $x \to \infty$: the limit is $\frac{4}{3} = \frac{4}{3}$. Taking the ratio of the constant terms, $-\frac{2}{7}$, is the classic slip.

variants.statement: Evaluate $\lim_{x \to \infty}\frac{7x^2 - 2x + 1}{2x^2 + 3x + 4}$.
variants.explanation: The degrees match, so only the leading terms survive as $x \to \infty$: the limit is $\frac{7}{2} = \frac{7}{2}$. Taking the ratio of the constant terms, $\frac{1}{4}$, is the classic slip.

variants.statement: Evaluate $\lim_{x \to \infty}\frac{9x^2 + 2x - 4}{3x^2 - x + 2}$.
variants.explanation: The degrees match, so only the leading terms survive as $x \to \infty$: the limit is $\frac{9}{3} = 3$. Taking the ratio of the constant terms, $-2$, is the classic slip.

variants.statement: Evaluate $\lim_{x \to \infty}\frac{3x + 2}{4x^2 - x + 5}$.
variants.explanation: The denominator's degree is higher, so it grows faster and the ratio dies to $0$. Taking the ratio of the leading coefficients, $\frac{3}{4}$, ignores the degree mismatch — that rule only applies when the degrees are equal.

variants.statement: Evaluate $\lim_{x \to \infty}\frac{2x - 3}{5x^2 + x + 4}$.
variants.explanation: The denominator's degree is higher, so it grows faster and the ratio dies to $0$. Taking the ratio of the leading coefficients, $\frac{2}{5}$, ignores the degree mismatch — that rule only applies when the degrees are equal.

variants.statement: Evaluate $\lim_{x \to \infty}\frac{5x + 1}{2x^2 + 3x - 4}$.
variants.explanation: The denominator's degree is higher, so it grows faster and the ratio dies to $0$. Taking the ratio of the leading coefficients, $\frac{5}{2}$, ignores the degree mismatch — that rule only applies when the degrees are equal.

variants.statement: Evaluate $\lim_{x \to \infty}\frac{4x - 1}{3x^2 + 2x + 6}$.
variants.explanation: The denominator's degree is higher, so it grows faster and the ratio dies to $0$. Taking the ratio of the leading coefficients, $\frac{4}{3}$, ignores the degree mismatch — that rule only applies when the degrees are equal.

forms.title: Choosing c for continuity
forms.skill: Continuity at the hole forces f(k) to equal the limit — cancel and substitute.

variants.statement: For what value of $c$ is $f$ continuous, where $f(x) = \frac{x^2 - 9}{x - 3}$ for $x \neq 3$ and $f(3) = c$?
variants.explanation: Continuity forces $c = \lim_{x \to 3} f(x)$; cancel the factor $x - 3$ so the limit is $x + 3 \to 6$. Choosing $c = 0$ because the raw formula looks like $\frac{0}{0}$ is the classic slip.

variants.statement: For what value of $c$ is $f$ continuous, where $f(x) = \frac{x^2 - 16}{x - 4}$ for $x \neq 4$ and $f(4) = c$?
variants.explanation: Continuity forces $c = \lim_{x \to 4} f(x)$; cancel the factor $x - 4$ so the limit is $x + 4 \to 8$. Choosing $c = 0$ because the raw formula looks like $\frac{0}{0}$ is the classic slip.

variants.statement: For what value of $c$ is $f$ continuous, where $f(x) = \frac{x^2 - 25}{x - 5}$ for $x \neq 5$ and $f(5) = c$?
variants.explanation: Continuity forces $c = \lim_{x \to 5} f(x)$; cancel the factor $x - 5$ so the limit is $x + 5 \to 10$. Choosing $c = 0$ because the raw formula looks like $\frac{0}{0}$ is the classic slip.

variants.statement: For what value of $c$ is $f$ continuous, where $f(x) = \frac{x^2 - 36}{x - 6}$ for $x \neq 6$ and $f(6) = c$?
variants.explanation: Continuity forces $c = \lim_{x \to 6} f(x)$; cancel the factor $x - 6$ so the limit is $x + 6 \to 12$. Choosing $c = 0$ because the raw formula looks like $\frac{0}{0}$ is the classic slip.

variants.statement: For what value of $c$ is $f$ continuous, where $f(x) = \frac{x^2 - 49}{x - 7}$ for $x \neq 7$ and $f(7) = c$?
variants.explanation: Continuity forces $c = \lim_{x \to 7} f(x)$; cancel the factor $x - 7$ so the limit is $x + 7 \to 14$. Choosing $c = 0$ because the raw formula looks like $\frac{0}{0}$ is the classic slip.

variants.statement: For what value of $c$ is $f$ continuous, where $f(x) = \frac{x^2 - 64}{x - 8}$ for $x \neq 8$ and $f(8) = c$?
variants.explanation: Continuity forces $c = \lim_{x \to 8} f(x)$; cancel the factor $x - 8$ so the limit is $x + 8 \to 16$. Choosing $c = 0$ because the raw formula looks like $\frac{0}{0}$ is the classic slip.

variants.statement: For what value of $c$ is $f$ continuous, where $f(x) = \frac{x^2 - 4}{x + 2}$ for $x \neq -2$ and $f(-2) = c$?

forms.title: The derivative as a function
forms.skill: Differentiate term by term: multiply by the exponent, drop it by one, kill constants.

variants.explanation: Bring each exponent down as a factor and lower it by one; the constant $5$ differentiates to zero. Leaving the coefficients un-multiplied, as in $2x^2 + 3x - 4$, is the classic slip.

variants.statement: If $f(x) = x^3 - 2x^2 + 3x - 6$, what is $f'(x)$?
variants.explanation: Bring each exponent down as a factor and lower it by one; the constant $-6$ differentiates to zero. Leaving the coefficients un-multiplied, as in $x^2 - 2x + 3$, is the classic slip.

variants.statement: If $f(x) = 3x^3 + x^2 + 2x - 4$, what is $f'(x)$?

variants.statement: If $f(x) = 2x^3 - 5x^2 + x + 3$, what is $f'(x)$?
variants.explanation: Bring each exponent down as a factor and lower it by one; the constant $3$ differentiates to zero. Leaving the coefficients un-multiplied, as in $2x^2 - 5x + 1$, is the classic slip.

variants.statement: If $f(x) = x^3 + 4x^2 - 2x + 7$, what is $f'(x)$?

forms.title: Power rule at a point
forms.skill: Bring the exponent down, lower it by one, then substitute the point.

variants.explanation: Power rule: $f'(x) = 4x + 3$, so $f'(2) = 11$. Computing the function value $f(2) = 14$ instead of the derivative is the classic mix-up.
Power rule: $f'(x) = 6x - 4$, so $f'(2) = 8$. Computing the function value $f(2) = 4$ instead of the derivative is the classic mix-up.
Power rule: $f'(x) = 6x^2 + 5$, so $f'(2) = 29$. Computing the function value $f(2) = 26$ instead of the derivative is the classic mix-up.
Power rule: $f'(x) = 8x - 3$, so $f'(3) = 21$. Computing the function value $f(3) = 27$ instead of the derivative is the classic mix-up.
Power rule: $f'(x) = 4x - 5$, so $f'(3) = 7$. Computing the function value $f(3) = 3$ instead of the derivative is the classic mix-up.
Power rule: $f'(x) = 9x^2 + 2$, so $f'(2) = 38$. Computing the function value $f(2) = 28$ instead of the derivative is the classic mix-up.
Power rule: $f'(x) = 10x + 4$, so $f'(2) = 24$. Computing the function value $f(2) = 28$ instead of the derivative is the classic mix-up.
Power rule: $f'(x) = 6x^2 - 1$, so $f'(3) = 53$. Computing the function value $f(3) = 51$ instead of the derivative is the classic mix-up.
Power rule: $f'(x) = 6x + 5$, so $f'(-2) = -7$. Computing the function value $f(-2) = 2$ instead of the derivative is the classic mix-up.
Power rule: $f'(x) = 8x + 1$, so $f'(2) = 17$. Computing the function value $f(2) = 18$ instead of the derivative is the classic mix-up.
Power rule: $f'(x) = 4x + 7$, so $f'(4) = 23$. Computing the function value $f(4) = 60$ instead of the derivative is the classic mix-up.

forms.title: Slope of the tangent line
forms.skill: The tangent's slope is the derivative evaluated at the point — not the y-value.

variants.statement: What is the slope of the tangent line to $y = x^2 + 4x + 1$ at $x = 2$?
variants.explanation: The slope of the tangent is the derivative: $y' = 2x + 4$, so at $x = 2$ the slope is $8$. Reporting the $y$-value $13$ instead of the derivative is the classic slip.

variants.statement: What is the slope of the tangent line to $y = x^2 - 3x + 2$ at $x = 4$?
variants.explanation: The slope of the tangent is the derivative: $y' = 2x - 3$, so at $x = 4$ the slope is $5$. Reporting the $y$-value $6$ instead of the derivative is the classic slip.

variants.explanation: The slope of the tangent is the derivative: $y' = 2x - 6$, so at $x = 4$ the slope is $2$. Reporting the $y$-value $-6$ instead of the derivative is the classic slip.

forms.title: Chain rule on a power
forms.skill: Differentiate the outside, then multiply by the inner derivative.

variants.explanation: Chain rule: $f'(x) = 2(2x + 1)^{1} \cdot 2$, and the inner value at $x = 1$ is $3$, so $f'(1) = 2 \cdot 3^{1} \cdot 2 = 12$. Forgetting the inner derivative $2$ gives $6$.
Chain rule: $f'(x) = 2(3x - 2)^{1} \cdot 3$, and the inner value at $x = 2$ is $4$, so $f'(2) = 2 \cdot 4^{1} \cdot 3 = 24$. Forgetting the inner derivative $3$ gives $8$.
Chain rule: $f'(x) = 2(2x + 3)^{1} \cdot 2$, and the inner value at $x = 1$ is $5$, so $f'(1) = 2 \cdot 5^{1} \cdot 2 = 20$. Forgetting the inner derivative $2$ gives $10$.
Chain rule: $f'(x) = 2(3x + 1)^{1} \cdot 3$, and the inner value at $x = 1$ is $4$, so $f'(1) = 2 \cdot 4^{1} \cdot 3 = 24$. Forgetting the inner derivative $3$ gives $8$.

forms.title: Acceleration: the second derivative

variants.statement: A particle's position is $s(t) = t^3 + 2t^2 - 5t$. Find the acceleration $s''(1)$.
variants.explanation: Differentiate twice: $s''(t) = 6t + 4$, so $s''(1) = 10$. Stopping after one derivative — the velocity $s'(1) = 2$ — is the classic slip.

forms.title: Product rule from a table
forms.skill: (fg)' = f'g + fg' — never the product of the derivatives.

variants.explanation: Product rule: $h'(1) = f'(1)g(1) + f(1)g'(1) = (3)(4) + (2)(5) = 22$. Multiplying the two derivatives, $(3)(5) = 15$, is the classic wrong move.
Product rule: $h'(2) = f'(2)g(2) + f(2)g'(2) = (-1)(2) + (3)(4) = 10$. Multiplying the two derivatives, $(-1)(4) = -4$, is the classic wrong move.
Product rule: $h'(3) = f'(3)g(3) + f(3)g'(3) = (2)(-3) + (4)(1) = -2$. Multiplying the two derivatives, $(2)(1) = 2$, is the classic wrong move.
Product rule: $h'(0) = f'(0)g(0) + f(0)g'(0) = (5)(3) + (-2)(1) = 13$. Multiplying the two derivatives, $(5)(1) = 5$, is the classic wrong move.
Product rule: $h'(2) = f'(2)g(2) + f(2)g'(2) = (-3)(2) + (5)(-1) = -11$. Multiplying the two derivatives, $(-3)(-1) = 3$, is the classic wrong move.
Product rule: $h'(1) = f'(1)g(1) + f(1)g'(1) = (2)(4) + (-3)(2) = 2$. Multiplying the two derivatives, $(2)(2) = 4$, is the classic wrong move.
Product rule: $h'(4) = f'(4)g(4) + f(4)g'(4) = (6)(2) + (1)(-3) = 9$. Multiplying the two derivatives, $(6)(-3) = -18$, is the classic wrong move.
Product rule: $h'(2) = f'(2)g(2) + f(2)g'(2) = (1)(5) + (-4)(3) = -7$. Multiplying the two derivatives, $(1)(3) = 3$, is the classic wrong move.
Product rule: $h'(3) = f'(3)g(3) + f(3)g'(3) = (-2)(1) + (6)(4) = 22$. Multiplying the two derivatives, $(-2)(4) = -8$, is the classic wrong move.
Product rule: $h'(1) = f'(1)g(1) + f(1)g'(1) = (3)(-2) + (7)(1) = 1$. Multiplying the two derivatives, $(3)(1) = 3$, is the classic wrong move.
Product rule: $h'(2) = f'(2)g(2) + f(2)g'(2) = (4)(3) + (-1)(5) = 7$. Multiplying the two derivatives, $(4)(5) = 20$, is the classic wrong move.
Product rule: $h'(5) = f'(5)g(5) + f(5)g'(5) = (-3)(4) + (2)(1) = -10$. Multiplying the two derivatives, $(-3)(1) = -3$, is the classic wrong move.

forms.title: Locating critical points
forms.skill: Set f'(x) = 0 and solve; the second derivative certifies a minimum.

variants.statement: For $f(x) = x^2 - 6x + 5$, at which value of $x$ does $f$ reach its minimum?
variants.explanation: Set $f'(x) = 2x - 6 = 0$ to get $x = 3$; $f''(x) = 2 > 0$ confirms a minimum. Flipping the sign to $x = -3$ — misreading the middle coefficient — is the classic slip.

variants.statement: For $f(x) = x^2 + 4x + 7$, at which value of $x$ does $f$ reach its minimum?
variants.explanation: Set $f'(x) = 2x + 4 = 0$ to get $x = -2$; $f''(x) = 2 > 0$ confirms a minimum. Flipping the sign to $x = 2$ — misreading the middle coefficient — is the classic slip.

variants.statement: The function $f(x) = x^3 - 3x$ has two critical points. What is the positive one?
variants.explanation: Solve $f'(x) = 3x^2 - 3 = 0$: $x^2 = 1$, so $x = \pm 1$ and the positive one is $1$. Answering $-1$ — the other critical point — misses the word positive.

variants.explanation: $f'(x) = 3x^2 - 12 = 3(x - 2)(x + 2)$ is negative exactly between the roots, so $f$ decreases on $(-2;\ 2)$. The outer intervals are where $f'$ is positive — where $f$ increases.

forms.title: Where the tangent meets the y-axis

variants.statement: Find the $y$-coordinate of the point where the tangent line to $y = x^2$ at $x = 1$ crosses the $y$-axis.
variants.explanation: The tangent at $x = 1$ is $y = 2(x - (1)) + 1$; setting $x = 0$ gives $y = -1$. Answering $1$ — the height of the point of tangency — is the classic slip.

forms.title: Picking the antiderivative

variants.statement: Which of the following is an antiderivative of $f(x) = 6x^2 + 5$?
variants.explanation: Raise each exponent by one and divide by the new exponent: $\frac{6}{3}x^3 + 5x$ differentiates back to $f(x)$. Differentiating instead of integrating — the classic reversal — gives $12x$.

forms.title: Definite integrals of linear functions
forms.skill: Antidifferentiate, then compute F(k) − F(0) — the Fundamental Theorem.

variants.statement: Evaluate $\int_0^4 (2x + 3)\,dx$.
variants.explanation: An antiderivative is $F(x) = x^2 + 3x$, so the integral is $F(4) - F(0) = 28$. Forgetting to halve the $x^2$ term gives $44$.

variants.statement: Evaluate $\int_0^3 (4x - 2)\,dx$.
variants.explanation: An antiderivative is $F(x) = 2x^2 - 2x$, so the integral is $F(3) - F(0) = 12$. Forgetting to halve the $x^2$ term gives $30$.

variants.statement: Evaluate $\int_0^3 (2x + 5)\,dx$.
variants.explanation: An antiderivative is $F(x) = x^2 + 5x$, so the integral is $F(3) - F(0) = 24$. Forgetting to halve the $x^2$ term gives $33$.

variants.statement: Evaluate $\int_0^2 (6x - 1)\,dx$.
variants.explanation: An antiderivative is $F(x) = 3x^2 - x$, so the integral is $F(2) - F(0) = 10$. Forgetting to halve the $x^2$ term gives $22$.

variants.statement: Evaluate $\int_0^4 (3x + 2)\,dx$.
variants.explanation: An antiderivative is $F(x) = \frac{3}{2}x^2 + 2x$, so the integral is $F(4) - F(0) = 32$. Forgetting to halve the $x^2$ term gives $56$.

variants.statement: Evaluate $\int_0^5 (2x - 3)\,dx$.
variants.explanation: An antiderivative is $F(x) = x^2 - 3x$, so the integral is $F(5) - F(0) = 10$. Forgetting to halve the $x^2$ term gives $35$.

variants.statement: Evaluate $\int_0^2 (4x + 1)\,dx$.
variants.explanation: An antiderivative is $F(x) = 2x^2 + x$, so the integral is $F(2) - F(0) = 10$. Forgetting to halve the $x^2$ term gives $18$.

variants.statement: Evaluate $\int_0^2 (2x + 7)\,dx$.

variants.statement: Evaluate $\int_0^2 (3x - 2)\,dx$.

forms.title: Area under a graph
forms.skill: Area under y = mx is a triangle (½·k·mk) — the integral agrees; under x² it is k³/3.

variants.statement: Find the area under the line $y = 2x$ from $x = 0$ to $x = 6$.
variants.explanation: The region is a triangle with base $6$ and height $2 \cdot 6 = 12$, so the area is $\frac{1}{2} \cdot 6 \cdot 12 = 36$ — exactly what $\int_0^6 2x\,dx$ gives. Forgetting the $\frac{1}{2}$ gives $72$.

variants.statement: Find the area under the parabola $y = x^2$ from $x = 0$ to $x = 1$.
variants.explanation: $\int_0^1 x^2\,dx = \frac{x^3}{3}\Big|_0^1 = \frac{1}{3}$. Forgetting to divide by $3$ gives $1$.
